steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures around the blank
steps.content: Before the blank is an independent clause: "Researchers studying magnetosensation have determined why some soil-dwelling roundworms in the Southern Hemisphere move in the opposite direction of Earth's magnetic field when searching for food." After the blank is another independent clause: "in the Northern Hemisphere, the magnetic field points down...but in the Southern Hemisphere, it points up, toward the surface and away from worms' food sources." The task is to choose punctuation that correctly joins two independent clauses where the second explains the first.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for explanation
steps.content:
A colon can join two independent clauses when the second elaborates on or explains the first. Here, the magnetic field information explains why the worms move opposite to the field when searching for food—because field orientation differs between hemispheres.

  • A: Colon correctly joins two independent clauses and signals that the second explains the first.
  • B: A comma cannot join two independent clauses on its own—that creates a comma splice.
  • C: "while" turns the second clause into a subordinate clause, but the resulting sentence is awkward and redundant since "when searching for" already establishes the temporal frame.
  • D: No punctuation between two independent clauses produces a run-on sentence.
